text: Hello, this is a case of a representative laparoscopic cholecystectomy. This was in a young female with symptoms of biliary colic secondary to gallstones.
The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar at the umbilicus and three five millimeter trocars in the upper abdomen, demonstrating entry into the abdominal cavity with the five millimeter trocars.
The laparoscopic camera provides a view from the twelve millimeter trocar positioned at the umbilicus, offering a perspective of the surgical field during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon grasps the fundus of the gallbladder and retracts it upward toward the head, revealing the liver, gallbladder, stomach, and duodenum in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses surgical instruments to carefully dissect and remove adhesions between the omentum, mesentery, and liver, clearing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon opens the anterior and posterior peritoneal reflections at the infundibulum of the gallbladder to expose the critical structures for the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection onto the liver bed on both sides, using the laparoscopic view from the umbilical trocar to visualize the gallbladder and surrounding structures.
The surgeon dissects the cystic artery high on the body of the gallbladder, using careful technique to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection high on the gallbladder, carefully maintaining distance from the portal structures to prevent injury to the common bile duct or hepatic artery.

The surgeon clears the inferior edge of the gallbladder, isolating the cystic duct and cystic artery. A Maryland dissector is used to dissect behind the cystic artery, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clears the area behind the cystic duct at its junction with the infundibulum of the gallbladder, further exposing the critical structures for dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection to achieve the critical view of safety, ensuring clear visualization of the cystic duct and artery, and their separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues dissection further up onto the cystic plate, where the gallbladder meets the liver bed, achieving the critical view of safety.
The surgeon presents the critical view of safety, showing the cystic duct and cystic artery entering the gallbladder, with the inferior edge of the gallbladder cleared up to the cystic plate.

The surgeon exposes the common bile duct while maintaining the critical view of safety, ensuring clear visualization of anatomical structures to minimize the risk of injury to the common bile duct or hepatic artery.
A stone is visible within the cystic duct. A single clip is placed laterally on the cystic duct, which is then partially opened.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to extract a stone from the cystic duct and subsequently remove it from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon places three additional clips medially on the cystic duct, securing it before further dissection.
The surgeon places two clips medially and one clip laterally on the cystic artery, securing it in preparation for division.
The surgeon identifies the node of Collot on the cystic artery, a nodular structure commonly found in this anatomical location. The cystic duct and artery are visibly divided.
The surgeon ensures complete hemostasis and removes the gallbladder from the liver bed using electrocautery, carefully detaching it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon places the gallbladder into an endocatch bag for removal from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon verifies complete hemostasis in the surgical field, ensuring there is no active bleeding following the removal of the gallbladder.
The surgeon irrigates the operative field, ensuring the area is clean and free of debris following the division of the cystic duct and artery.
Thank you.
